script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Friday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed down one point four two percent on the session.

Intel and Micron both posted deep single-session losses Friday, and the damage was not isolated. The semiconductor complex sold off broadly, pulling the portfolio below the flatline and raising hard questions about what is coming next for chip-dependent names heading into a critical earnings week.

Markets closed mixed Friday, with the Nasdaq underperforming as chip stocks bore the brunt of investor anxiety over heavy artificial intelligence spending ahead of major tech earnings reports. The Dow edged higher while the S&P five hundred finished nearly flat, reflecting a rotation away from high-multiple technology names rather than any broad-based deterioration in risk sentiment.

Intel was the single worst performer in the primary portfolio Friday, falling seven point eight nine percent on the session. That kind of move on no single dramatic headline is its own signal — it reflects accumulated anxiety around the company's competitive positioning in both the data center and AI accelerator markets, where it continues to cede ground to rivals. Intel carries the smallest weight among the nine primary holdings, at two point nine percent of the portfolio, which limits the direct damage. But the stock's persistent underperformance is a drag that compounds over time, and with no near-term catalyst clearly visible, the pressure warrants close attention. Micron Technology also fell sharply, down six point nine nine percent, adding to the semiconductor pressure. Together these two names were responsible for the majority of Friday's portfolio loss.

Moving through the full holdings rundown now, beginning with the largest positions.

Nvidia fell zero point nine two percent on the session. The broader chip sector weakness weighed on the stock despite Nvidia's continued dominance in AI accelerator demand. What to watch is whether institutional buyers step in at current levels ahead of the next major data center spending disclosures from hyperscalers next week.

Apple was up three point five three percent on the session, the strongest performer in the primary portfolio Friday by a significant margin. Investor's Business Daily flagged Apple with its highest possible composite rating of ninety-nine, highlighting the stock as a swing trade setup in the megacap universe. Apple's ecosystem stickiness and consistent consumer loyalty remain the core bull thesis. Earnings are on the near-term horizon, and that report will be the next major test.

Turning to Alphabet, which gained zero point six five percent Friday. Seeking Alpha issued a strong buy rating with a base case price target of four hundred twenty-six dollars and sixty-eight cents, citing robust earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization growth. Separately, Alphabet closed a dark-fiber agreement with Verizon valued at more than one billion dollars, reinforcing its artificial intelligence infrastructure buildout. Alphabet kicked off Big Tech earnings this week and raised its capital expenditure guidance — Wall Street is now watching whether that spending translates into revenue acceleration.

Over at Microsoft, the stock was essentially unchanged, up zero point zero three percent on the session. Microsoft reports earnings in the coming days, making this a critical inflection point. AI integration across its cloud and productivity platforms remains the central thesis.

Amazon fell zero point six six percent Friday. The stock drifted lower alongside broader consumer discretionary weakness and ongoing investor caution about AI spending returns. Earnings are approaching and will be closely watched for cloud growth signals.

Advanced Micro Devices dropped three point two nine percent on the session. The stock was caught in the same semiconductor downdraft that hit the broader chip sector Friday. Competitive positioning in the AI GPU market relative to Nvidia remains the key long-term question for holders.

Tesla declined two point zero eight percent Friday. The stock has faced persistent valuation pressure as analysts reassess the Magnificent Seven complex broadly. No specific company news drove Friday's move — the decline reflects sector-level rotation out of high-multiple names.

Intel, as covered in the top story, fell seven point eight nine percent. It remains the weakest performer among primary holdings on both a session and trend basis.

On the secondary side, Sandisk was the notable mover, falling ten point seven nine percent — the sharpest single-session decline across all tracked names Friday. Applied Materials dropped four point seven two percent, and Lam Research fell four point five six percent, confirming that semiconductor equipment names were under broad pressure across the session.

Three items to track in the next forty-eight hours. First, Apple earnings — expected early next week — will set the tone for the largest single position in this portfolio and for consumer technology sentiment broadly. Second, Microsoft earnings arrive on the same timeline and carry direct read-through implications for enterprise cloud and artificial intelligence adoption. Third, Amazon's earnings report will be scrutinized for signals on cloud revenue trajectory and AI capital return timelines — both matters directly relevant to this portfolio's positioning.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch for Apple and Microsoft earnings results, which have the potential to reprice a significant portion of this portfolio in either direction.
